cards.question: What is the time complexity of inserting at the head of a Linked List?
cards.answer: O(1) — constant time. We only update the head pointer, regardless of list size.

cards.question: What does an Array guarantee that Linked Lists do not?
cards.answer: Contiguous memory allocation, which enables O(1) random access by index via pointer arithmetic.

cards.question: What is the difference between singly and doubly linked lists?
cards.answer: A doubly linked list has pointers to both the NEXT and PREVIOUS nodes, enabling bidirectional traversal at the cost of extra memory.

cards.question: When should you prefer a Linked List over an Array?
cards.answer: When you need frequent insertions/deletions at the beginning or middle, and do NOT require random index access.

cards.question: What is the space complexity of a singly linked list with n elements?
cards.answer: O(n) — each node stores the data plus one pointer to the next node.
